title: Transport Detection of Whirlpools in GaAs Electron Liquid
authors: Dmitry A. Egorov, Dmitriy A. Pokhabov, Evgeny Yu. Zhdanov, Andrey A. Shevyrin, Askhat K. Bakarov, Arthur G. Pogosov
abstract: We report the formation of large-scale steady-state whirlpools in a GaAs-based two-dimensional electron liquid and demonstrate them by straightforward transport measurements. A whirlpool forming inside a circular cavity adjoining a wide conducting channel appears as a negative four-terminal resistance over a broad range of temperatures and cavity sizes. The effect scales with the Gurzhi length, in quantitative accord with the hydrodynamic analogy. Obtained results firmly establish this analogy and probe the limits of its applicability.
summary: This paper reports the experimental detection of steady-state electron whirlpools in a GaAs-based 2D electron liquid using transport measurements. By showing that the appearance of negative resistance scales with the Gurzhi length, the authors provide strong evidence for hydrodynamic flow and probe the limits of the hydrodynamic analogy.
main_problem: The difficulty of directly observing and characterizing hydrodynamic vortices (electron whirlpools) in electron liquids and distinguishing them from ballistic transport.
main_result: The authors demonstrate the formation of large-scale steady-state whirlpools in a GaAs electron liquid, showing that the transition to negative resistance scales universally with the Gurzhi length (D/D_nu approx 8.0).
method: The study combines four-terminal transport measurements in GaAs/AlGaAs heterostructures with numerical hydrodynamic simulations and scaling analysis.
model_or_system: A high-mobility two-dimensional electron gas (2DEG) in a GaAs/AlGaAs heterostructure, featuring a device geometry with circular cavities adjoining a wide conducting channel.
key_observables: Four-terminal resistance (R), potential difference (V), and current streamlines.
important_parameters: Gurzhi length (D_nu), cavity diameter (D), temperature (T), electron density (n), and mobility (mu).
main_assumptions: The hydrodynamic model assumes a slip boundary condition consistent with previously reported perfect slip flow in GaAs.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows device schematics and simulated current/potential maps; Fig 2 shows potential difference; Fig 3 shows the scaling of Gurzhi length with cavity diameter; Fig 4 shows resistance in a reference geometry without vortices.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of vortex detection, describes the GaAs experimental platform and device geometry, presents transport measurements and scaling analysis, compares results to numerical simulations, and concludes with a discussion on the breakdown of standard viscosity relations.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks
